title: How to Apply for Caste Certificate Online — SC/ST/OBC 2026
meta_description: Caste certificate application 2026 — how to apply SC/ST/OBC online state-wise. Documents needed, state portals, processing time 15-30 days.

hero.one_liner: Caste certificate (SC/ST/OBC) is essential for availing 15-27% reservation in government jobs and education. Apply online through your state e-District portal in 3 steps.

sections.heading: Why Caste Certificate is Critical
content: A caste certificate is official proof that you belong to a scheduled caste (SC), scheduled tribe (ST), or other backward class (OBC) as recognized by the central or state government. It's the gateway to availing reservation benefits in government jobs, educational institutions, and government schemes.

Without a valid caste certificate, you cannot: (1) Apply for SC/ST/OBC reserved positions in government jobs (50-100% of recruits may be unreserved/general category if they don't have caste certificates), (2) Get admission under reserved quota in colleges/universities (85-90% seats reserved for state domicile students, of which some are further reserved for SC/ST/OBC), (3) Avail SC/ST/OBC-specific scholarships and grants, (4) Apply for priority schemes like Stand Up India (SC/ST entrepreneurs), PM Mudra (SC/ST priority).
The certificate itself is free in most states (or costs ₹10-50), but the value it unlocks is enormous — a difference of ₹5-10 lakh in scholarship benefits or job selection.

sections.heading: SC vs ST vs OBC — Key Differences
rows: Castes historically considered 'untouchable' and socially excluded
Indigenous tribes with distinct culture and language
Castes socially/educationally backward but not SC/ST

sections.heading: Understanding OBC-Creamy Layer vs Non-Creamy Layer
content: Important: For central government jobs and central institutions (IITs, NITs, AIIMS), OBC candidates must have both: (1) OBC certificate, AND (2) Non-Creamy Layer (NCL) certificate. OBC certificate alone is insufficient.

What is 'Creamy Layer'? An OBC person is in the creamy layer (and ineligible for OBC reservation at central level) if: (a) Any parent holds/held a Group A/B central government post for 10+ years, OR (b) Annual family income from non-agricultural sources exceeds ₹8 lakh/year for past 3 years.
Salary income alone (even above ₹8L) does NOT make you creamy layer under most recent DoPT guidelines.

Non-Creamy Layer certificate: Confirms that you satisfy both conditions: (1) OBC caste is recognized, (2) Family income is below ₹8L from non-salary/non-agricultural sources. Valid for 1 year — must be renewed annually because income can change.

States vs Central: For state government jobs and state universities, some states only require OBC certificate (not NCL). Always check whether you're applying for central or state jobs — requirements differ.

sections.heading: How to Apply for Caste Certificate Online

steps.title: Visit your state's e-District or revenue portal
steps.description: Each state has its own portal for caste certificate applications: UP (edistrict.up.gov.in), Bihar (serviceonline.bihar.gov.in), MP (mpedistrict.gov.in), Delhi (edistrict.delhigovt.nic.in), Karnataka (ipass.karnataka.gov.in), Maharashtra (aaplesarkar.mahaonline.gov.in), etc. Google '[Your State Name] caste certificate online apply' to find the exact portal.

steps.title: Register and fill application form
steps.description: Create an account or login. Select 'Caste Certificate' service under 'Revenue Department' or 'Administrative Services'. Fill: (1) Your full name (as on Aadhaar), (2) Father's full name, (3) Mother's name, (4) Date of birth, (5) Current address, (6) Your caste/sub-caste (check central OBC/SC/ST list first), (7) Category (SC/ST/OBC), (8) Purpose of certificate (for job, education, scheme, etc.).

steps.title: Upload supporting documents
steps.description: Prepare scanned copies: (1) Aadhaar card (first 2 pages), (2) Father's caste certificate (if available — this significantly speeds up approval), (3) School leaving certificate (must mention caste/category — critical!), (4) Ration card (shows family members), (5) Self-declaration affidavit on ₹10 stamp paper ('I certify that I belong to [caste] of [SC/ST/OBC] category'), (6) Passport-size photo, (7) Domicile certificate (some states require this). If father's certificate not available, add: community members' affidavit (3-4 people from your community), land records from Patwari office (showing your caste).

steps.title: Pay fee and submit
steps.description: Fee is ₹10-50 depending on state. Pay online via net banking/debit card/UPI through the portal. Click 'Submit' and note down the reference/tracking number. You'll receive SMS/email confirmation with your application number.

steps.title: Patwari/Revenue officer verifies your claim
steps.description: After submission, the Patwari (or Lekhpal in some states) of your revenue circle visits your home/village to verify: (1) Is the family actually known as [caste] in the community? (2) Do revenue records (old land records, property tax documents) show your caste? (3) Do neighbors/community members confirm your caste? This physical verification is crucial — don't ignore any notices from the Patwari. If caste is disputed, multiple affidavits from community elders strengthen your case.

steps.title: SDM approves and certificate is issued
steps.description: After Patwari's report, the Sub-Divisional Magistrate (SDM) or Tehsildar approves your application. Certificate is issued and you can: (1) Download from the portal (PDF, digitally signed), (2) Collect from Tehsil office, (3) Get delivered via DigiLocker in some states. Processing time: 15-30 days total.

sections.heading: Critical Documents Needed
rows: Proof your family is already recognized as [caste] by government; fastest approval method
Get affidavits from 3-4 community members + land records from Patwari's office showing family caste
Official record showing your caste in school records
If school record doesn't exist, get Tehsil office to confirm from old village records, or use community affidavits
Must have Aadhaar for any government service in 2026
Use voter ID or electricity bill as alternative
Your written statement declaring your caste
Must be on ₹10 stamp paper, signed by you with thumb impression if illiterate
Neighbors/community elders confirm your caste identity
Get 3-4 local people (same community) to write on ₹10 stamp paper + affix thumb impression

sections.heading: State-Wise Caste Certificate Portals
content: North India: UP (edistrict.up.gov.in), Bihar (serviceonline.bihar.gov.in), Himachal (himachal.nic.in), Punjab (punjab.gov.in), Delhi (edistrict.delhigovt.nic.in)
Central India: MP (mpedistrict.gov.in), Chhattisgarh (cgstate.gov.in), Jharkhand (jharkhand.gov.in)
South India: Karnataka (ipass.karnataka.gov.in), Tamil Nadu (tnega.tn.gov.in), Telangana (meeseva.telangana.gov.in), Andhra Pradesh (mparthasarathi.ap.nic.in)
West India: Maharashtra (aaplesarkar.mahaonline.gov.in), Gujarat (gujinet.nic.in), Rajasthan (emitra.rajasthan.gov.in)

sections.heading: If Your Caste Certificate Gets Rejected
content: Reason 1: 'Caste not recognized' — Your caste is not on the central SC/ST/OBC list. Solution: Check the official NCBC list (ncbc.nic.in).

Some castes are on state lists but not central lists — then you get state SC/ST/OBC benefits but not central. If your caste is missing from both lists, you cannot get caste certificate; contact your caste association to petition for recognition.

Reason 2: 'Patwari verification failed' — The Patwari couldn't confirm your caste identity from records or community. Solution: Get stronger community evidence: (a) Get 5-6 community members to sign affidavits, (b) Get caste organization certificate confirming you're member, (c) Provide historical land records showing family's caste from 20+ years ago (from revenue office).

Reason 3: 'Name mismatch with school records' — Your name on Aadhaar doesn't match old school records. Solution: Either update your Aadhaar name (₹50, 10-30 days) to match old records, or provide an affidavit explaining the name change (marriage, nickname correction, etc.).
Get the Patwari to note that both names refer to the same person.

Reason 4: 'Domicile mismatch' — You're applying in State A but school records/property records show you're from State B. Solution: Get a domicile certificate or provide documents showing you've shifted to current state.
Some states require 3-10 years residency proof before issuing caste certificate to migrants.

sections.heading: Special Cases & FAQs
content: Q: My father's caste is different from what I know. How do I prove my caste? Caste is generally patrilineal — you inherit father's caste.

If your father's caste certificate shows [Caste A] but you believe you're [Caste B], you have two options: (1) File with father's caste using his certificate, (2) Get your father's caste certificate updated first (this is rare and requires proof). It's usually easier to go with father's established caste.
Q: My parents are from different states. Which state do I apply in? Apply in the state where you're currently domiciled (where you have Aadhaar, voter ID, ration card).
Some institutions may accept certificates from either parent's state of origin, but current state of residence is standard.
Q: I'm adopted and don't have father's certificate. Can I still get caste certificate? Yes.

Use adoptive father's caste if you were adopted into that caste. Provide adoption documents + adoptive father's caste certificate (if available) + community affidavits confirming you're accepted as that caste.
Biological caste doesn't matter legally once you're adopted and accepted by the community.

sections.heading: External Links & Resources
content: Official Lists:
National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) — Official OBC List
National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC) — SC List
National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (NCST) — ST List
State Portals:
UP e-District (edistrict.up.gov.in) | Bihar RTPS (serviceonline.bihar.gov.in) | MP e-District (mpedistrict.gov.in)

faqs.question: Is caste certificate valid for lifetime or does it expire?
faqs.answer: SC/ST caste certificates are valid for lifetime in most states. OBC certificates are also lifetime, but OBC-NCL (Non-Creamy Layer) certificates are valid for only 1 year and must be renewed annually. Check your specific state's rules and the certificate itself — it should mention validity period. For central government jobs, you don't need recent certificate as long as it was issued by a competent authority (SDM/Tehsildar).

faqs.question: My school doesn't mention caste on my leaving certificate. How do I prove caste?
faqs.answer: School records are the easiest proof, but not the only way. If your school certificate doesn't mention caste (which is common for students from urban/private schools), use: (1) Father's caste certificate, (2) Ration card showing your category, (3) Land records from Patwari showing family caste, (4) Affidavits from 4-5 community members. The Patwari will verify from revenue office records that date back 20-30 years.

faqs.question: I belong to a caste that's OBC in my state but General in another state. Which category applies?
faqs.answer: You follow your state of domicile's caste list. If you're domiciled in State A where your caste is OBC, you get OBC status. If you move to State B where the same caste is not OBC, you don't get OBC benefits in State B. For central jobs, you must follow the central OBC list. Always check which list applies to your situation before applying.

faqs.question: Can I get caste certificate if my father is from one state and I'm born in another?
faqs.answer: Yes. You can get caste certificate in either: (1) Your father's state of origin (using his caste certificate), or (2) Your current domicile state (using Aadhaar and residency proof). If you apply in a different state from your father's, you may need additional documents like domicile proof. Applying in your current state of residence is usually easier.

faqs.question: What's the difference between 'Caste Certificate' and 'Community Certificate'?
faqs.answer: 'Caste Certificate' is the formal government-issued document confirming your SC/ST/OBC status. 'Community Certificate' is sometimes issued by gram panchayat or community leaders (informal). For official purposes (government jobs, education), you need the formal caste certificate from SDM/Tehsildar, not community certificate. However, community certificate can be useful initial proof while applying for formal caste certificate.

faqs.question: I'm applying for UPSC and need OBC-NCL certificate. My state hasn't renewed it since last year. What do I do?
faqs.answer: For UPSC (and most central recruitments), the OBC-NCL certificate must be valid on the notification date or closing date of application. If your certificate expired, immediately apply for renewal in your state portal. Processing takes 7-15 days. Apply urgently — if not received by closing date, you cannot claim OBC benefit for that exam. Plan ahead and renew before expiry.

faqs.question: The Patwari says my caste is not in records. I've lived here 20 years. What now?
faqs.answer: If revenue records don't show your caste but you've lived in the community for 20 years, get strong community evidence: (1) Get 5-6 neighbors/community members (preferably from your caste community) to sign affidavits on ₹10 stamp paper, (2) Get gram panchayat's statement confirming you're part of the community, (3) Show long residence: ration card, property records, children's school records from the area. Resubmit with these documents. The Tehsildar has discretion to issue based on community acceptance.

faqs.question: Can I apply for caste certificate while my father's certificate is being processed?
faqs.answer: It's possible but slower. If you apply without father's certificate, the Patwari will take longer to verify your caste (20-30 days instead of 7-15 days). Wait for father's certificate to be issued first if possible — it significantly speeds up your approval. If urgent, apply without waiting but understand the delay.

disclaimer: Caste certificate procedures and OBC/SC/ST lists vary by state and central government. This guide is based on common practices as of March 2026. Always verify specific requirements with your state's revenue department.
